method3.generated_article:
==Name==
The island's name is commonly given as Brecqhou, with Brechou recorded as an alternative spelling.[5] In modern usage, “Brecqhou” refers specifically to the small private island west of Great Sark, whereas “Sark” may refer both to the island of Sark and to the wider jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou and nearby islets and rocks.[1][5] Historically, Brecqhou was also known as Isle des Marchants after ownership passed through the Le Marchant family following the marriage of Jeanne de Chesney to Denis Le Marchant of Guernsey.[2] The name Isle des Marchants continued to be used even after ownership of the island later passed to others.[2]

==Geography==
Brecqhou, also spelled Brechou, is one of the smaller inhabited Channel Islands in the English Channel and forms part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[2][3] It is associated geographically and jurisdictionally with Sark, which lies within the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[1][4] The island is privately owned and has a very small population compared with the main island of Sark.[1][4] Brecqhou is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[4] The Sark–Brecqhou grouping is part of a wider Channel Islands archipelago located off the coast of Normandy in north-western France.[2][3] Geologically, Brecqhou shares the ancient basement rocks of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, with metamorphic rocks, chiefly schists and gneisses, forming most of Sark and Brecqhou.[5] These rocks are among the oldest in the Channel Islands and record early Proterozoic deformation and metamorphism dated to about 2,020 million years ago.[5]

==Flag and stamps==
Flags and other symbolic markers associated with Brecqhou have been discussed mainly in the context of the island’s contested relationship with Sark, rather than as symbols of a recognised separate polity.[2][4] Available flag-related material referring to Brecqhou includes online popular-symbolism references to the island, but the cited material does not establish the design or official status of a Brecqhou flag.[1] Postal issues connected with Brecqhou are set against the wider postal history of Guernsey, whose postal administration was formally established in 1794 and whose postal independence from 1969 allowed distinctive local stamp issues.[5] This postal framework reflects Guernsey’s constitutional position as a British Crown dependency while retaining historical links with the British postal system.[5] Academic treatments of Sark and Brecqhou therefore place questions of symbols and postal identity within the wider politics of jurisdiction, ownership and local authority in the Sark–Brecqhou relationship.[2][4]

==Public visits==
Brecqhou is a privately owned island off the west coast of Sark, and public access is restricted to visits by invitation only.[1][4] The island was sold to the Barclay brothers in 1993, and later descriptions of access state that visitation is not open to the general public.[4] This contrasts with nearby Herm, which is described as the smallest of the Channel Islands open to the public.[1]

==Notes and references==
Administrative and legal sources should be read carefully because references to Sark as a jurisdiction may include Brecqhou unless the two islands are distinguished separately.[2]

Published island-studies literature gives Brecqhou’s area as about 160 acres and notes that its proximity to Sark, combined with its surrounding sea cliffs, has shaped discussion of its separateness and accessibility.[2]

Cartographic references for Brecqhou commonly rely on locally produced Channel Islands mapping rather than Ordnance Survey coverage, including Bailiwick of Guernsey maps that include Sark, Jethou and Brecqhou.[4]

Sources discussing Brecqhou identity often treat flags, carriage labels and stamps as expressions of separateness within a contested relationship with Sark rather than as evidence of sovereign status.[2]

Recent political accounts of Sark frequently mention Brecqhou in connection with the Barclay brothers, litigation, electoral reform and debates over development and local autonomy.[3]

Natural-history and travel accounts provide incidental observations of Brecqhou’s coastline, nearby waters and views from Sark, but they are less central than legal, geographic and island-studies sources for constitutional or ownership questions.[5]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
Sark’s feudal relationship with Brecqhou is usually traced to the 1565 grant by Elizabeth I to Helier de Carteret, under which Sark was held as a fief haubert and the Seigneur owed personal homage and military service to the Crown.[2] The grant required de Carteret to defend Sark, and later interpretations have treated Brecqhou as falling within the same feudal sphere because of its connection with Sark’s fief and its defensive obligations.[1]

Brecqhou’s precise feudal status has nevertheless been contested, with Rivett arguing that it was not originally part of the Sark fief and was only later claimed as such.[1] Other accounts dispute whether Brecqhou was historically a true Sark tenement, including claims that Dame Sibyl Hathaway improperly conferred tenement status and a seat in Chief Pleas on the island.[4] By contrast, mid-20th-century legal advice prepared for a sale described Brecqhou as a dependency of Sark and stated that its tenure was similar to that of Sark’s other tenements, with the owner legally described as a tenant in perpetuity.[4]

Official descriptions have generally accepted Brecqhou as part of Sark’s jurisdiction, with the Ministry of Justice describing Sark as including Brecqhou and the British-Irish Council using similar language.[1] On that basis, Sark’s legislature, Chief Pleas, has treated Brecqhou as a tenement within the fief, so that Sark law applies there unless specifically disapplied.[1] The partial disapplication of some Sark laws has given Brecqhou a special position within the jurisdiction, contributing to disputes over whether the island is merely a tenement or enjoys a degree of autonomy greater than other Sark holdings.[1]

The modern dispute intensified after Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay bought Brecqhou in 1993 and became liable under Sark’s feudal tax rules to pay the treizième, a payment of one-thirteenth of the purchase price to the Seigneur.[3] Their legal and political campaigns challenged aspects of Sark’s feudal system, helping to overturn male-preference inheritance rules and contributing to the replacement of the treizième in 2007 with a property transfer tax payable to the island government.[3] The Barclays also supported media campaigning for democratic reform on Sark, and the Privy Council subsequently approved universal voting rights and a 28-member elected assembly.[5] Sark’s feudal government was replaced by an elected assembly in 2008, although the wider question of Brecqhou’s place within Sark’s jurisdiction has continued to be discussed in terms of history, law and political power.[1]

==Fort Brecqhou==
Fort Brecqhou was designed by the British architect John Quinlan Terry and built between 1994 and 1996 as a private country house for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay.[2] The DiCamillo Companion describes it as “the largest house built in Britain in the last 200 years”.[2] The building is a granite-clad castle on a quadrangular plan in a castellated Gothic style, with circular turrets at each of its four corners.[2] It also has large twisted chimneys in a Tudor-derived style associated with the reign of Henry VIII.[2] Contemporary descriptions note that the structure was built using two types of Spanish granite and was intended to have the character of a traditional fortress rather than a modernist residence.[1] The estimated cost of constructing the house, gardens and outbuildings was £90 million.[2] The house is not listed, and its primary use is as a private home, with access described by the DiCamillo Companion as limited to the grounds by appointment.[2] Peter J. Rivett’s history of the island, *Brecqhou: A Very Private Island*, includes an account of the Barclays’ acquisition period and the building of Fort Brecqhou.[3]
